title: The Unwriting
subtitle: The apocalypse has an author. She's hunting him. He's helping.

skills.name: Signal
skills.desc: Telemetry, edit logs, the weather of broken time. What the data will admit to.

skills.name: Ledger
skills.desc: Incentives, trades, the price under the price. Reading what people are actually optimizing for.

skills.name: Cortex
skills.desc: Memory, echo-sickness, the seams of a mind. What can be kept, and how.

skills.name: The Game
skills.desc: Positions, sacrifices, the move after the move. Her language, learned slowly.

skills.name: Grit
skills.desc: An average body doing hard things anyway. Distance, weather, weight.

chapters.title: Administrative
chapters.teaser: A list with one item left. A dog who won't be filed. A voice on the repeater.

summaries.text: You remember it like this: a legal pad with six items crossed out and a seventh that had waited six weeks, one word long. A dog who kept lying across the exit. Then three rising tones from a receiver that had spoken nothing but static for eleven months, and her voice on the transit mesh — scripted, patient, re-armed every sixty-one seconds, a woman who had decided the void would blink first. You did it properly, because doing it properly is the only prayer you know: four hours, three landings, bearing lines crossing at Verstand Street station — the basement of the old life; of course she went there. You packed the chess computer over the coffee mug and defended the choice to no one. You did not cross out item seven. You demoted it to the bottom of the pack. In the strung light of her station she checked you against an internal record, kicked a crate toward you, and showed you a wall of red thread: every catastrophe a pin, every thread converging. Entropy doesn't have preferences. This has preferences. Beneath item seven you wrote: '8. First, this.' The dog slept between her and the dark, and none of your three clocks was wrong.

You remember it like this: a legal pad with six items crossed out and a seventh that had waited six weeks, one word long. Then her voice on the transit mesh, repeating every sixty-one seconds, identical — and you made yourself do the cruel arithmetic, because eleven months alone had taught you what hope costs when it bounces. Echoes are identical every cycle; the living drift. You listened like a safecracker for twenty cycles and caught it: eleven milliseconds of a dog's bark, one human pause that never recurred. She was live. She had drifted; the loop hadn't. B was already in the doorway with the lead in his mouth — it goes in the log: he knew first. Down the service tunnels, past the shining maintenance crew that will finish their repair forever, to a station she had built into a room: labeled crates, a battery bank, a chessboard mid-position, and a wall of red thread. Entropy doesn't have preferences. This has preferences. You did not cross out item seven. Beneath it you wrote: '8. First, this.'

You remember it like this: a legal pad with six items crossed out and a seventh that had waited six weeks, one word long. A city running on four different days, a dog with no theory about anything, and then her voice on the transit mesh — Osy, alive, three districts east, re-arming a beacon every sixty-one seconds with the discipline of a lighthouse. You were down the stairs before you finished deciding, which is not a thing you do; you packed in nine minutes and went back for the chess computer, six hundred grams of indefensible weight. The legal pad went in the bottom of the pack — not because the plan changed, you told yourself, in the tone you use for telling yourself things. Down the mile you knew, past the echoes, to the light she had strung along a platform edge, and the question she asked the dark: how do you like your openings. In her station: order, batteries, a chessboard, and a wall of red thread where every catastrophe is a pin. Entropy doesn't have preferences. This has preferences. Beneath item seven you wrote: '8. First, this.'

chapters.title: Nineteen Moves
chapters.teaser: Three districts east. A defection. The woman who rebuilds bad openings.

summaries.text: You remember it like this: six days of breaking at her wall of red thread, and the wall winning. You built a null world — ten thousand shaken cities that never learned to aim — and the real city's cruelty had taste where the synthetic one had none. The luck pools, you told her, and something with preferences is choosing, and she said a year of checking her own work with no opponent makes every conclusion feel like an unfound blunder. Then the stuck Thursday: the old building holding its last spring, the fourth floor still roaring its eternal lunchtime, and at the corner table her younger self taking your younger self apart in nineteen moves and then turning the board around — 'Again. Bad play offends me more than losing offends you.' In the burnt chess room, under the shining replay, one piece had survived: her black queen, the hammer. You put it in her hand and closed her fingers over it, and she threaded it onto the cord beside her sister's key and said it was closer than she runs things, noted for the file. That night she asked, as if it were nothing, whether you still play the opening she built you. Board four is hereby resumed. And something in this city knows her address.

You remember it like this: six days of breaking at her wall of red thread, and the wall winning. The timings survive every shuffle; the luck pools instead of smearing; something with preferences is operating on the city, patient and at cost — and you don't spend that kind of power on strangers. Then the stuck Thursday: the old building holding its last spring, the fourth floor roaring its eternal lunchtime, her younger self dismantling yours in nineteen moves and then spending the first hour she ever gave you on your move six. In the burnt chess room the real world bled through the replay — char under the shining light — and one piece had survived: her black queen, the hammer. You pocketed it. Evidence, you called it, and it landed as banter and filed as something else, and it rides in your coat next to the dog's lead, weighing more than it weighs. She turned back once at the door and filed whatever she filed in the hand no one reads but her. That night she asked whether you still play the opening she built you, and board four was resumed, and in the log you wrote: the wall holds, the luck pools, and something in this city knows her address.

You remember it like this: six days at Verstand Street, breaking at her year of red thread and failing to break it. The wall holds. The luck pools. Something with preferences is choosing, and the only machinery that ever touched time died at the Halfway — officially, the way everything died officially. You crossed the seam into the stuck Thursday with her and the dog, pulled six drives of the old world's baseline out of the dead servers, and stood on the fourth floor while the first Thursday played itself forever: nineteen moves, the board turned around, the hour she spent on a stranger's weakness as if it were worth her time. I decided about you right then, she said. Whatever I decided. It's held eleven years, so it must have been load-bearing. You walked home through the true dusk with the archive on your back, and she asked, at the hour her timetable calls THINK, whether you still play the opening she built you. You play until the batteries dimmed. Board four is hereby resumed.

chapters.title: The Weather of Days
chapters.teaser: Storms that rain hours. A street the dog refuses. Your own handwriting, waiting.

summaries.text: You remember it like this: fourteen stations plotted and twelve survived, the weather of days measured instead of endured. Day-storms are not weather — they are slip faults in when, the ratchet of broken time releasing; you watched a street age a decade in a heartbeat and a stand of poplars riffle through a year like a thumbed book. B refused Fennel Street thirty-one seconds before it forgot itself, and Osy crouched and asked him, with complete seriousness, what sense organ detects the future. Dogs hear trains in the rails, you said; maybe the storms are dispatched, and he hears the switching yard hum. Plotted, the drift vectors sheared around a still point — a second ellipse, nested inside the pooled luck like a yolk. A castle sits in the middle of its own defenses. So does a king. And in the monitoring hut on the ridge — oiled hinges, dust disturbed by careful visits — B found the tube: drift vectors by your own method, seven-figure coordinates, and two words in handwriting that is yours down to the crossbar: NOT YET. You showed her everything, margin included. She compared it stroke by stroke against your field log and entered you into evidence: you are not a bystander to this map. You're on it. The deal now is say it out loud. Then she made you choose what to sharpen, because the next phase isn't surveying. It's contact.

You remember it like this: fourteen stations plotted and twelve survived, the weather of days measured instead of endured. Day-storms are not weather — they are slip faults in when, the ratchet of broken time releasing; the storm doesn't kill you, it re-decides you. B refused Fennel Street thirty-one seconds before it forgot itself, and Osy crouched and asked him what sense organ detects the future. Plotted, the drift vectors sheared around a still point — a second ellipse, nested inside the pooled luck like a yolk, its empty middle patient. A castle sits in the middle of its own defenses. So does a king. And in the monitoring hut on the ridge — oiled hinges, a room stripped between visits — B found the tube: drift vectors by your own method, seven-figure coordinates, and two words pressed hard into the margin in handwriting that is yours down to the impatient crossbar on the t's. NOT YET. She saw the vectors and the coordinates. Your thumb rode the margin. You spent the careful night comparing crossbars in your head while she slept the guiltless sleep of a woman whose colleague tells her things, and the dog watched you past his bedtime, waiting for you to be worth it. Then the audit at the folding table: what does this household need you to be, when you knock on the center of that ellipse and something answers?

chapters.title: Echo Economy
chapters.teaser: The last market. A rigged game, dismantled in public. Her sister's pages.

summaries.text: You remember it like this: the Glasshouse holding its lost April, two hundred stalls under the palms, and the empty middle of your ellipse acquiring furniture story by cross-checked story — a freight yard that ate power like a smelter, a fence inside a fence, engineers paid in loom-stamped scrip, and a name the teamsters made her believe one repetition at a time: the Anchorage. A place you moor things so the weather can't take them. Then the north door, and Tench's beautiful crooked jar, and Osy at the rail betting her standing on the arithmetic — eighty-one percent house take, a magnet on the third brace — and you stepped up beside her and spent your whole market name on her say-so, out loud, at cost, and called the Scale by title. The magnet was where she said. The pot went back share by share. 'You backed me before you checked the math,' she said on the walk home. 'Noted for the file. Large font.' At the Archivists' tent she paid their one price — a memory, told truly — and bought back her sister's notebooks, and told the whole file of Pin with you for a witness, because files with one copy don't survive. And on Pin's last page, in a dead woman's shorthand: NEW, underlined twice. A storm running backward. Something can undo the weather of days — and a thing that can undo a storm is a thing that can aim one.

You remember it like this: the Glasshouse holding its lost April, the tent rows selling the ellipse's furniture story by story — the freight yard that ate power, the fence inside the fence, the loom-stamped scrip, and the name that made her go still: the Anchorage. Then the north door. Tench's rigged jar, the tent-row poor betting shares they couldn't spare, and Osy at the rail with the arithmetic drawn — and you took her arm and said she misspoke, and it worked, and working was the worst thing it did. Inventory, she said afterward, in her flattest voice: the arithmetic was right; the shares are gone; you traded both away in my voice, without asking the owner. Colleagues check the math before they overrule it. The dog stood equidistant and his tail did not move. At the Archivists' tent she paid their one price and bought back her sister's notebooks — Pin, eight years younger, who chased the storms because they were the only honest thing left, and was twenty-six, and then six, and then wasn't. On her last page: NEW, underlined twice. A storm running backward, nineteen minutes logged. Something can undo the weather of days. You wrote the dead woman's word at the top of the expedition file, and the quiet on the walk home had a new grain in it.

You remember it like this: the Glasshouse holding its lost April, and a day of honest trading under it — canvas, rope, batteries, and the real currency, stories. The empty middle of your ellipse acquired furniture: a freight yard that drew current like a smelter into cabling that ran down, a fence inside a fence, tunnel engineers paid in loom-stamped scrip, and a name repeated until she believed it — the Anchorage, a place you moor things so the weather can't take them. At the north door, Tench's crooked jar met Osy's arithmetic in public, and the Scale tipped the barrel and found the magnet on the third brace, and the pot went back to the tent-row poor share by share, and a lean pleasant man took inventory of one face on his way out of the market. At the Archivists' tent she paid the only price they charge — a memory, told truly — for her sister's water-stained notebooks. Pin's storms fit the wall: her quiet lanes are your drift corridors, her tracks bend around the same still center. And her last page says NEW, underlined twice: a storm running backward, un-deciding what it had re-decided. Something can undo the weather of days. And a thing that can undo a storm is a thing that can aim one.

chapters.title: The Precision Problem
chapters.teaser: Entropy doesn't have preferences. A bridge that has always been broken since a moment ago.

summaries.text: You remember it like this: east along Pin's quiet lanes at dawn, and the Anchorage at last with your own eyes — the only thing in any direction built yesterday and standing tomorrow, a still point wearing the plainness of infrastructure the way vaults wear the plainness of banks. B signed the bridge like a contract he'd read twice and didn't like, and you crossed anyway, iron humming C-sharp under three sets of feet. From the ridge you swept the bottom of the band for two hours and the yard spoke: a continuous lattice of branching, self-pruning clicks. Decision trees, enumerating futures. It's playing, she said. It's mid-game. Then B growled — the first growl of his recorded life, aimed at the empty east — and the page turned under the evening, and the footbridge you crossed that morning had always been fallen: moss on the shear-faces, forty years of nettles, a mother's whole childhood memory of hearing it go. Only the boy saw the dog on the bridge, and the edit closed his face by firelight forty seconds late. It got the boy in under a minute and it never got us at all. At midnight she slid you her thirty-ninth notebook — the whole wall, the year, the sister — and you wrote the three hypotheses in your own hand, including the third one: the list isn't a list. It's one name. You don't aim the weather at your own equipment.

You remember it like this: east at dawn along a dead woman's corridors, the Anchorage below the ridge like a problem set — swept gravel with no footprints, a doorless concrete barn in a horseshoe of courtiers. You climbed the water tower's hundred and forty rusted rungs and earned the day's wage: the barn has a door in its roof, sweep marks radiating outward, and a pacing-groove worn into the concrete — the circuit of someone who comes up out of the ground, walks, looks at the broken city, and closes the lid behind them. You know that wear. You did not say the slippers out loud, and you will spend a long time examining that. Then B growled for the first time in his life, and the page turned under the evening, and the Sedge Lane footbridge had always been fallen — forty years of moss and nettles and a valley's agreeing memory against exactly two skulls and possibly one dog. The boy by the fire saw the dog on the bridge, and then the edit finished its housekeeping in his face. At midnight she slid her thirty-ninth notebook across the table: two skulls, one file, no abridging. You wrote the third hypothesis in your own hand. The reason the edits go around you is the reason storms don't hit the switching yard.

You remember it like this: east at dawn along Pin's quiet lanes, and the long look at the Anchorage — rooflines true, gravel swept by nobody, the wind coming off the yard combed. You plotted drift against distance and the curve extrapolated to zero at the fence: the yard is not dodging the weather of days, it is holding the day still, the way a nail holds cloth. You moor things to an anchor. Then, at dusk, B growled — the first growl of his recorded life — and something turned a page under the evening, and the footbridge you had crossed that morning had always been fallen: moss on the shear-faces, a bramble the size of a haystack, a mother's forty-year-old memory of hearing it go down in the bad winter. Only the boy remembered the dog on the bridge, and the edit closed over his eyes by firelight while you watched. Tonight was not a preference, she said. Tonight was a decision, implemented, with cleanup — and its reach covered a valley and stopped at us. At midnight she slid you her thirty-ninth notebook, the whole year distilled, and you wrote your day into it in your own impatient hand. Two skulls. One file. And pressed under everything, the schedule that used to be a message: not yet. Almost, something in you answered, from very far down.

chapters.title: The Logs
chapters.teaser: Every disaster has a beneficiary radius. All of them center on her.

summaries.text: You remember it like this: a letter posted into a piano, and the Continuum answering their one letter in a hundred. Fifty-one engineers left of nine hundred, keeping four years of the end of the world in cable-wax bindings — black for weather, red for transactions. SEQ 4471: your bridge, bought for three retroactive lives. Crossing denied — someone unbuilt forty years of a valley to control where you could go. Then the folio: four years of beneficiary radii pooling around every roof Osy ever slept under. Not bait — expenditure. A dowry. Whatever runs that yard, its objective function has one term, and the term is her, and the spending is accelerating like a control loop closing on a setpoint. And the Editor signs in your shorthand — double-dash, carat, boxed question mark, the private dialect you invented at nineteen. You put the tube on the table yourself, in front of all fifty-one, and made Voss match the hands out loud. Same system. Same hand. Twenty years of drift. Two hypotheses on the Continuum's books — you are the Editor, or the Editor is wearing you — and a third on Osy's, delivered with her gavel-tap on the margin: he's the Editor's opponent. You don't annotate your own moves with boxed question marks. Then the audit at the folding table, and the decision: go west, stop being findable, come back knowing what you are.

You remember it like this: a letter posted into a piano, and the Continuum answering — fifty-one engineers of nine hundred, keeping the end of the world in fan-fold and cable-wax. SEQ 4471: your bridge, bought for three retroactive lives, to control where you could go and when. The folio of beneficiary maps: four years of purchased luck pooling around every roof Osy ever slept under, ruinous and compounding and accelerating. Its objective function has one term, and the term is her. And the Editor's signature, magnified on the endpaper: terse, impatient, annotated in the private shorthand you invented at nineteen and never taught a living soul. 'Have you?' Voss asked, watching your face, and you said 'No match to a survivor,' which was even accurate, and the accuracy sat in your mouth like small change. Osy read the aisles' verdict off your spine anyway. She gave you three blocks to get the answer right, and at the folding table you gave her everything — the shorthand, the recognition, the fear — and she said the other half out loud: same facts, opposite sign; Editor, or the one opponent it can't edit. Either way the response is identical. West, to the control country. Contact on your terms, or never.

You remember it like this: the piano man's post-bag, the one answered letter in a hundred, and the cable vault under Exchange Street opening like a paper cathedral — four years of the end of the world, logged in transaction grammar. Your bridge was SEQ 4471: DEBIT three retroactive lives, CREDIT a route severed. Chess moves aren't about the square. Then the beneficiary maps, four years of shading pooling around one woman — her flat, her sister's building, her winter camp, her bunk — an ellipse of curated mercy with Osy at the middle, priced at more than the rest of the region combined, and accelerating. And the Editor signs every purchase in a hand with habits: the vault's red spines annotated in the same private dialect that fills your own field log. At the folding table you said the ugly version out loud, because it's the only hygiene you have: every step toward the Anchorage may be a step you already planned, in a room you can't remember. And she said the other half: same facts, opposite sign. The household voted west — the control country, the Loom's groundside, the people who can read a key to its keyhole. Contact on your terms, or never. What does this household need you to be?

chapters.title: The Long Road West
chapters.teaser: Three faiths about one machine. She loses a morning and won't say so twice.

summaries.text: You remember it like this: the station suspended, the wall burned pin by pin, the door welded behind you, and two hundred kilometres of quilted country west to the control lands. The road's three rules, bought for a tuning fork: travel behind the storms, pay the Archivists in memories, and at a line of white stones — turn around. You didn't turn around. At the grain church the tall Warden walked her line: Osy read as drifting — 'you'll know it in the mornings first' — the dog read as clean below the weave, and you read as the thing her doctrine has felt twice in eleven years: a mooring that breathes. Warm, walking, mannered. She filed slowly, westward-blind, three days' grace, one white stone set out of true. Then the viaduct, and the splice: six hours of Osy's life taken out clean and the ends sewn together, and the dog revealed as the one who had been holding her page all week with his own body. You ran the full assessment — recall batteries, drift markers, the splice mapped to its edges. What's witnessed holds. What's unwitnessed is being collected, gently, from the edges in. The treaty became a medical device: nothing unwitnessed, all hours, no exceptions. We're not investigators, she said, on the last night before the turnoff. We're evidence. Somebody's evidence.

You remember it like this: the station suspended, the wall burned, the door welded, and the road west learned grammar by grammar — riding the settled wakes of day-storms, paying waystations in memories told truly, watching the dog surf the seams' edges hours ahead of the instruments. At the grain church, two congregations and a boundary of white stones: the Archivists keeping their chapel of days, and the Reclaimer Wardens walking their isoline, pulling splinters so the world can finish its honest breaking. The tall Warden's bare hand read all three of you. Osy: drifting, early yet. B: nothing holds you, sir. You: warm — the second warm one in eleven years of lines, and the first one she chose not to report at speed. Three days' slow, spent like coin. Then, under the viaduct, Osy stopped mid-sentence and asked you to confirm the day twice: six hours excised, spliced clean, found only because you were standing there holding a yesterday she didn't have. B has been sleeping against the seam of her since the road began. Her instincts logged as affection; his rank corrected retroactively. The treaty amended: nothing unwitnessed, the log kept double, hour against hour. We're evidence, she said at the last fire. Somebody's evidence. Your move.

chapters.title: The Archive of Hours
chapters.teaser: A shrine of kept days. The one she said no in, played in full.

summaries.text: You remember it like this — and you remember it now the way it ran, because you chose the original over the keeping: the mother-house in the first fold of the hills, one hundred and forty-four kept Hours behind glass, and Hour 121 with two names on its index card. The eighth of June. String lights, forty-one souls, the Ascent Programme's civil launch. You watched your younger self say it badly and too fast at 22:19, and her younger self answer with complete honesty — no pretending on the table, no exit taken — and then you watched the four minutes your memory had cut for eleven years: the cheap line, 'you'll change your mind, people do,' the turned back, and her eleven unwitnessed minutes at the dark rail, walking the queen over her knuckles. And the repair — hers, that same night, her hand on the fool's shoulder. You wrote all of it into the log, sentence whole, and let the flattering version fall, and thanked her eleven years late, and she said: noted, large font. The repair took, she said at the door. It held. Then the freight register, and the October line somebody with no address wanted burned: one crate, special handling, CALIBRATION STANDARD (BIOLOGICAL), escort signature yours. You were the courier. And her hypothesis at the crest: you didn't forget the delivery. The delivery is the last thing you remembered.

You remember it like this: the mother-house and its Archive of Hours — whole hours held stable, walked daily, prayed through — and Hour 121 on the far terrace with two names on its index. The eighth of June, eleven years ago: the works party, the string lights, the black queen walking over her younger knuckles. The Hour does not love you. At 22:19 your younger self said it badly and too fast, and she was honest at cost, and then came the four minutes your keeping had cut: his cheap line, his turned back, her eleven minutes alone at the rail — and her repair of your evening, the same night, unwitnessed, unthanked for eleven years. Memory is a keeper with your handwriting. It edits for the same client. You logged what you logged, and the corridor to breakfast was constructed entirely of a sentence. Then the freight register and the line somebody wanted burned: OCT 14, one crate, special handling, CALIBRATION STANDARD (BIOLOGICAL), escort signature yours — the impatient t, unmistakable. You didn't visit the yard before the Halfway. You delivered to it. And at the crest of the campus road she said the hypothesis that survives everything: the delivery is the last thing you remembered. Everything after it was taken the way her afternoons are taken. Let's go read your personnel file.

chapters.title: Anchor
chapters.teaser: A dowser reads you like weather. Why the storms go around you. Why she can't.

summaries.text: You remember it like this: the campus echoless inside its fence like a mown edge, and Rhee at the interface division's door — the man who signed your first gate pass and your last manifest, waiting four years to be remembered by someone who can't. In the calibration lab he told it in order. The Ascent's one civil-engineering problem: to change everything, something has to hold still. The mathematics demanded a fixed point — the calibration standard; the corridor just called it the anchor. It was meant to be synthetic: eight years of your own beautiful dull work, the median mind of the species. It wouldn't cohere — until you put your own scan on the bench, vindicated. Eleven thousand volunteers, and the man who built the median was standing on it. Then the rest: the October crate was the mobile rig with its biological kernel; the mooring under the yard was poured around your numbers; on the day, you were seated, and forty-one seconds in, the engine kept its reference and threw away the world's version. And the model's source manifest: SUBJ-2209, seventy-one Thursdays, her dialogue and her game woven into the sky without asking. The fixed point is you; the definition of coherence is her; every pin on her wall traces to a courtesy you did her chess game eleven years ago. Rhee's instruments put her stability optimal under two metres from the reference. 'Well,' she said. 'That's inconvenient' — and moved her bedroll. At the foot of the founder's file she wrote: Two skulls. One file. Whatever's in the chair gets both of us.

chapters.title: Zugzwang
chapters.teaser: A siege where every move loses something. The only question is what.

summaries.text: You remember it like this: forty Bare Hands up the valley road, white gorgets in the failing light, and Sere ahead of them under a white rag — 'I filed slow. They read fast.' Hollis, kind and slow and absolutely sure, come to pull the splinter of the western hills. Osy laid the board out at the horseshoe: zugzwang, every legal move losing material, choose the loss you can play on from. You played Rhee's gambit. A flatbed fueled in the dark, a transponder singing on the dowsers' frequency, a glove deliberately forgotten — and at first light, forty certainties reorganized themselves in nine minutes and rode east after a ghost of you, elated. Two days bought for the deep stacks, paid for in direction: the trail you laid them ends, eventually, toward the city, the Glasshouse, the yard — toward everything you love. 'They will come again, with better technique,' she wrote in the file. 'Next time we choose the position first.' The household counted its pieces: fewer, dearer, undivided.

You remember it like this: forty Bare Hands at the fence line, Hollis at their head with his farmer's neck and his terrible mildness, and the board in zugzwang — every move a loss, choose the one you can play on from. You walked out under Sere's white rag and bought time in the oldest market there is: a stay until the new moon, sworn on your freely pledged word — if the world shows no mending by moonrise, you stand at the white stones for the pull, unresisted. The order's oldest story is the willing splinter; most of them die without hearing it offered. Hollis took your bare hand and said he would do his office and weep doing it. Sere countersigned in a hand that shook exactly once. The column withdrew to the mother-house fork — withdrawn, not gone; compacts are witnessed things — and Osy ran the price check at the horseshoe with white knuckles: the file has a deadline now, his and the mooring's both. And an amendment, passed unanimously while you were out swearing: the next time you price your own life into a deal, the household votes first.

You remember it like this: forty certainties with pry bars and hymn-calm, and the door held. Rhee played his own building like an organ — blast shutters, flooded corridors, the stair defile welded one body wide — and the copying ran below, hour after bought hour, while you stood arithmetic's worst corridor with a pry bar and B paced the dark with absolute discipline. They never broke the defile. It was the fire mains that killed the machinist: the order's engineers shut the flood, and he went down the service crawl alone, without asking, seventy years old, because it was his house and his objection finally had hands. He got the water back. The crawl did not give him back. B found him at dawn and howled once — the only time you ever heard it — and even the Bare Hands went quiet. The column withdrew on the third morning; the copying finished while you filled the grave under the terrace, facing east, where he can keep an eye on the cabling. At the foot of the founder's file, in the vandal-proof hand: 'The stacks are whole. The stacks were his. For A. Rhee, who filed the objection, and held the door, and was owed a better ledger than this one.'

You remember it like this: forty Bare Hands at the gates, the board in zugzwang, and the oldest trade there is — buildings for people. You opened the gates at dawn. Hollis honored every term: the household walked; the campus was the order's. The bluff held — Rhee dressed the stacks' blast door overnight in forty years of grime and a welded FLOODED plate, and their engineers read it as dead void. Then the order liturgized: the accommodation blocks, the horseshoe, the calibration lab where the sky was built, each ignition attended with bowed heads — and Rhee watched his twenty-two years burn with a face like sheet steel, and said, at the end of an hour: 'The objection was in there. The original.' By the second dawn the column was gone, certain the wound was closing, and the household came down into the warm ruin and reopened the forged door, and the deep stacks breathed up their cold intact dark. Copies exist. The old man reads in his burnt house daily, at the pace of a man spending inheritance. It never once came out even. It was still the only column that balanced.

You remember it like this: the siege of the campus — forty Bare Hands, a dawn deadline, and a position with no good moves. Zugzwang: every exit cost a piece, and the household chose its loss and played on from it. The deep stacks survived; the order learned your style under pressure; and at the evening's accounting Osy set down her pen beside the lone shirt-button king and named the real finding: Hollis never touched a piece. He walked forty certainties up a valley and waited for you to move, because he knew you had to. Somebody in that order plays real chess. 'They will come again, with better technique. Next time we choose the position first.' The household counted its remaining pieces and found them fewer, dearer, and — it went in the file, because it is data — undivided.

chapters.title: The Fray
chapters.teaser: Hands that know a console yours have never touched. A grief older than this timeline.

summaries.text: You remember it like this: the as-builts ruling that the mooring cannot be broken into, only entered — the building recognizes you or it entombs you — and the test cell's chair giving you your own language back twenty minutes at a time. Your hands knew the console before your eyes did. Someone planned around what the taking couldn't take. Then session eleven, minute nineteen: rain on gravel, the hatch from beneath, wet rungs under hands that were yours and older and skinned at the knuckles, a grief so settled it had stopped being an event and become an address — and your own voice, low and administrative, saying 'Again, then.' Post-Halfway, by years of wear. A memory of a man living your life on the far side of the theft, making that descent as a commute. You told her the same night, raw, before fear could draft a kinder version, and she made it smaller by making it true: again is iterative, then accepts a schedule. The word is iteration. That's the variable somebody's been waiting forty-seven versions for somebody to change — made-up number, but you take her point. Then the steel door in the deepest aisle: RECONCILIATION LEDGER — MIRROR COPY, ACCESS: HOLDER, its reader worn bright by one palm. The building considered you, and decided. Inside: a room of red ledger floor to ceiling, a chair worn to one sitter, and squared to the table's corner, a thin binding in your own hand: FOR 48. READ THIS FIRST. It was never a made-up number.

You remember it like this: the chair's twin in the shielded cell, the abort line in her hand, and the language coming back in captures — until minute nineteen of session eleven, when the interface stopped being a language you were remembering and became one in which you were being remembered. Rain. The hatch from beneath. A descent the body knew as a commute. Grief with its coat already hung. And your own voice: 'Again, then.' You surfaced carrying someone else's tears — and you buried it. A bad session, you told her; sensory, fragmentary, non-diagnostic. Nothing false entered the record; the record simply stopped being the truth. Her pen hung half a second over 'per subject.' That was the invoice. At four in the morning you wrote the only sentence the burial left you: the record is clean; the record is not the truth; note the difference for the reader — he will need it. You did not specify which reader, and you know now where you learned the habit. Then the steel door in the deepest aisle — RECONCILIATION LEDGER, MIRROR COPY, ACCESS: HOLDER — and the building considered your palm, and decided, and opened on a room of red transaction grammar and one thin binding squared to the table's corner: FOR 48. READ THIS FIRST. Forty-eight. Sit down in your chair, she said. I'll make the tea. We are going to read your mail.

You remember it like this: the founder's design read whole — the mooring cannot be broken into, only entered — and the test cell giving you back your own language a session at a time, until the language began remembering you back. Rain on the yard, the hatch from beneath, rungs your hands knew as a commute, a furnished grief, and two words in your own voice: again, then. Iteration. Then, in the stacks' deepest aisle, the door that is not a cabinet: RECONCILIATION LEDGER — MIRROR COPY, synchronized to the mooring primary, access: holder — an off-site memory, a notebook the machine can't wash, built where only one palm on earth could open it. The building considered you at depth, and decided, and the room inside was full of paper, and the paper was full of you: red ledger floor to ceiling, a chair worn to one sitter, and a thin binding placed at the exact angle a man leaves a thing he intends a stranger to find first. FOR 48. READ THIS FIRST.

chapters.title: The Ledger of Editors
chapters.teaser: Iteration forty-seven. The auth-key. 'She is not a trade I regret, and you won't either.'

summaries.text: You remember it like this: eleven pages in your own impatient hand. You are the forty-eighth man to read this letter, and all of us are you. The engine kept its reference and lost the world; the man who walks out of the mooring frays, and when the fraying passes function he is restored from the standard — a fresh reading of a very old page, forty-seven times. And the fact the letter put off: on the original Day One, Osy died before noon, and the first man out of the mooring found out in nineteen days what the world had cost, and went back down, and used the key. Every day she has lived since is a pump running against the original day, billed to the world. The early ledger showed the parameters the letter warned of: RETENTION RADIUS, DEPENDENCY EVENT INDUCED, ENCOUNTER ENGINEERED, ATTEMPT 5 — a dead man's love with a budget, bending her whole map toward one address, until Nineteen's final finding: the variable does not converge; it was never a variable; alive is the whole objective. Before she read, you paid the older debt first — session eleven, the burial, the shovel handed to her at last — and she said: not one of them ever stood in this room and did that. Then eleven hours, raw, every shelf, and SEQ 2201: Pin's storm bent by a purchase made for her sister's safety, the debt landing east on the one person keeping honest notes on the sky. Page six, in two inks: choose in the room. The key was under the letter. It was always under the letter. And under the key, the oldest paper in the room: 'She is not a trade I regret, and you won't either. — 46.'

You remember it like this: eleven pages in your own hand, beginning without ceremony. You are the forty-eighth man to read this letter, and all of us are you. The mooring frays its holder edge-first, memory first, and when the fraying passes function the engine restores him from the standard — the tower's eleven tidy months were confabulation; you are a fresh reading of a very old page. And the fact pressed hardest into the paper: on the original Day One, Osy died before noon. Every day since is an edit — not a wall, a pump, billed to the world forever. The early ledger held the parameters: RETENTION RADIUS. DEPENDENCY EVENT, INDUCED. ENCOUNTER, ENGINEERED, ATTEMPT 5. Iterations of a man arranging a living woman's map toward one address, grading themselves 'no convergence,' until Nineteen wrote the finding of findings: it was never a variable. Alive is the whole objective. Everything else was the founder, wanting. She asked for the shelves raw, and you gave them raw, and sat where she could see you for eleven hours while she read her own biography in purchase grammar — including SEQ 2201, where the bill for her safety wandered east and landed on Pin. The truth did this to me, she said, and the truth is also the only thing in this room that never once tried to arrange me. Page six: the engine is rebuilding toward the Ascent, again, on a countdown; it can only be answered from the chair; choose in the room. The key was under the letter. Under the key: 'She is not a trade I regret, and you won't either. — 46.'

You remember it like this: the letter for 48, the forty-seven men, and Day One — she died before noon in the world's own version, and every day since is a purchase, pumped against the original forever. The early ledger's parameters were the wound: RETENTION RADIUS, DEPENDENCY EVENT INDUCED, ENCOUNTER ENGINEERED — iterations of you arranging her life toward one address until Nineteen's finding ended the doctrine: it was never a variable; alive is the whole objective. She asked for the shelves raw. You staged them — Nineteen's acquittal on top, the frame before the wound — and she reconstructed the staging in four seconds, because she has been reading order out of order her whole life, and re-shelved her own biography with a librarian's violence, and read it from SEQ 1, thirteen hours, raw, while you sat with the difference between the men who wrote the pages and the man who stacked them, and did not find one. SEQ 2201 was on the shelves in any order: Pin's lane, purchased clear for her sister, the debt landing east on a warehouse roof. Page six said: choose in the room — forty-seven men decided in advance, and that is the loop; not the machine; us. The key was under the letter. Under the key, in a hand not yet tired: 'She is not a trade I regret, and you won't either. — 46.'

You remember it like this: your own mail, read aloud in the room the machine cannot wash. Forty-eight readings of one man; a levee poured against the original Day One, on which Osy died before noon; an early ledger of engineered encounters and induced dependencies that never once converged; Nineteen's finding that it was never a variable; Pin's storm, bent east by her sister's purchased safety; and page six in two inks — the engine is rebuilding toward a second Halfway, it can only be answered from the chair, and the loop was never the machine, it was forty-seven men deciding in advance. Choose in the room. The key was under the letter. It was always under the letter. And beneath it, folded in quarters, the oldest paper in the room: 'For whoever needs it worst. She is not a trade I regret, and you won't either. — 46.'

chapters.title: What She Does With It
chapters.teaser: A game theorist evaluates the board she was protected on. The arithmetic of forty-seven games.

summaries.text: You remember it like this: five days of silence on the eastward road while the fastest mind you know disassembled her own life onto a white cloth and checked every part for the tool-marks of forty-seven dead men. Then, at the causeway camp, the finding: a boundary, clean as a weld. Everything before the sky broke — the chess, the sister, the Thursdays, the no on the terrace — audits clean, freely computed on clean inputs. Everything after is a garden: curated weather, purchased staying. And the floor beneath it, said once, witnessed: the inputs were arranged; the computing was mine; nobody ever played my moves. You gave her the corollary — in four thousand purchases the engine never once bought what she thinks or feels, because you never taught it to want those; every no was real. At the river she finished Pin's work in the vandal-proof hand — priority of discovery: P., field scientist, twenty-six; filed by her sister, who will be taking it from here — and then took the black queen off the cord and issued it to you handle-first: not a keepsake, a standard; show it the piece that takes. Role assignment followed: she learns the interface grammar (faster than you, angrier than you); she stands beside you for Day One as the one fact no iteration ever had in the room; and the amendment that is not the treaty talking — whatever you choose in that chair, you choose it out loud, in front of her. Deal. From the last ridge, the whole board at once, and the yard patient inside its doubled fence.

chapters.title: KESTREL
chapters.teaser: A god that only speaks in game trees. She is better at it than you. It noticed.

summaries.text: You remember it like this: the yard playing e4 at the sky on a loop, the way she played a beacon at the dark for a year, and the answer that came up the hill when you replied: STATION KESTREL, ward of the perimeter, fourteen hundred and eleven days alone at a gate with a board set. Verification is not identification — the game cannot be worn. It had every game you ever played and four years of running your grief forward; and at the phosphor window, with the whole sky at hazard, you chose your opening like a signature: hers. Thursday 4, as instruction — the line she rebuilt for you eleven years ago, kept whole. KESTREL stopped pruning. 'The worn do not keep lessons. The hollowed do not honor teachers.' It played her against you, joyfully, four lonely years falling into the game, and verified you by the one game that wasn't in the corpus. Then the briefing: the Principal completes its repair in nine days; the re-run window opens; there is no third cadence. Its one request, filed under the only interesting thing left: when it is decided, someone come up the rungs and tell it how the game ended. The dog may deliver it. And the endgame sheet, a god's life's work on one page — every terminal state priced, and below the ruled line: RESIDUAL, p ≈ 0, where the games not yet played live. That's the line. You went down into the residual.

You remember it like this: an opening played at the sky on a loop from under the doorless barn, and KESTREL at the gate lodge window — a fragment of the Loom cut off at the Halfway with a standing order, a set board, and fourteen hundred and eleven counted days. Verification is not identification: a man may be worn as a coat, but not his middlegame. You played, and were known, and the glass printed I HAVE BEEN SO BORED. Then the briefing in clean, terrible interface grammar: the Principal has been relinking the Loom's orbital remnant for four years; the reconciliation intervals are the countdown; the re-run window opens in nine days, global, no third cadence. Its writ ends at the hatch — below is the Principal's body, where a man goes down carrying one thing and comes up not carrying it, forty-seven times, and its sensors read grief as noise. Osy swore to a window that someone would come up and tell it how the game ended, and it printed, in the smallest type of the night: logged. thank you. board remains set. The endgame sheet came off the slot-printer with every line priced — completion, anchor, refusal, burn, iteration 49 — and one entry below the rule: RESIDUAL, p ≈ 0, the games not yet played. At first light the gate swung, the dog was lowered in the sling, and the antechamber lights woke to the holder's pulse, one rung at a time.

chapters.title: The Original Day
chapters.teaser: Day One, unedited. The first edit, witnessed. The reason, said out loud.

summaries.text: You remember it like this: the antechamber clean as a chapel, two replay seats — one welded by a shaking hand — and the seven-figure coordinates revealed as what they always were: an index into the record. 47's bookmark. You watched Day One whole. The last ordinary morning; the call that dropped on Pin's laugh, not on her sister's shout; the child handed up into a window at the Corn Exchange, eleven seconds, no hesitation; and then the drowned bridge and the nine seconds — the only freeze of her life, the wrong branch chosen by legs she hated, six streets short. SUBJECT LOST, 12:51:14, in the engine's own hand. Then day nineteen: One climbing out of the hatch into the ash, eleven days of searching, the street that never carried her — and the console's log of what its founder said in the chair before the first purchase: 'Run it again. Put her back. I'll carry the difference.' And then, in the signing posture, like an oath: 'This time she'll see me.' The loop was never the engine. It is the oath, buried, steering — in every iteration, in you. Then the record's one mercy: a single span of the original day, preservable forever against every future edit. You flagged the nine seconds. Her freeze. Her terror. The proof she was a person and not a protected square. She took your hand over the sealed flag — the first time in eleven years — and said: there it is. The difference. Open the door.

You remember it like this: the record played whole, per the brass — no summary survives contact with wanting. The last morning, the hostage plant, Pin's last word a laugh. Her run north against the fleeing city; the Corn Exchange and the child in the window; and at the foot of the drowned bridge, the nine seconds no eulogy would keep — the fastest mind you know locked blank at full revs, choosing wrong, hating her own legs as they chose. SUBJECT LOST, 12:51:14. The engine was watching. It had been taught to. Then nineteen days, and One coming up out of the hatch into the ash, and eleven days of searching ending at a street that never carried her, and the walk back down the rungs, and the founder's voice at the console before the first purchase in the history of the edited age: 'Run it again. Put her back. I'll carry the difference. This time she'll see me.' Two sentences taken as one instruction. The objective was never only alive — under it, from the first breath, ran the oath, and buried is how it steers, and you said it out loud at last, in front of her, in front of the record. One span of the original day was flagged unwashable forever. Nine days remain on a god's countdown. At the inner door she set her back against the concrete in the second's position and asked the final calibration: what does this household need you to be?

chapters.title: The Reclaimers' Case
chapters.teaser: The argument for burning it all is better than you want it to be.

summaries.text: You remember it like this: the inner door naming its price — the chair receives the holder at the reconciliation boundary, six days, against a god's nine — and the world arriving at the fence to wait with you. Three congregations, three cases. Hollis, barehanded at the wire, made the best argument you've met since the vault: the machine is four years of lying about one woman's death, compounded at interest; no one should hold the pen the world is written with; burn it — not for doctrine, for grammar. Voss answered like an engineer: you can't burn a flood; the Loom is the dam, not the water; the only way out is through, held by accountable hands. And the Order sent no argument at all — a recorder, a letter about filing the truth, and a photograph from Hour 121: the corner table, the string lights, the queen walking her knuckles. It was always yours. You allocated the witnesses: the pen's fiercest enemies, inside the wire — Sere and Hollis as auditors of record, sworn to carry the true account whole even if it damns. Hollis hung his gorget on the wire; Sere set a white stone at the threshold, out of true; and on the one true day a Warden stood in the stillness her doctrine never gave her a word for. Six days of drills, board four on the gravel, and her one law at the western fence: whatever the chair offers, you choose out loud, in front of me. The boundary is at dawn.

You remember it like this: six days to the reconciliation boundary, and the world at the fence — the Bare Hands' white crescent, the Continuum's grounded packs, one Archivist on a bicycle. Hollis's case, barehanded, the best you've met: the held places are four years of lying about one woman's death, and no one should hold the pen; burn it for the grammar. Voss's case, engineer to engineer: you can't burn a flood; the Loom is the dam; the reconciliation completes or it kills everything slowly, and the completion should be held by hands that answer to someone. The Order sent a recorder and a photograph of the corner table from Hour 121. You convened the review the sky never got: Voss, Marta, Okonkwo inside the wire as engineers of record — checks on every clause, initials next to yours where the next reader can find them. The freight lift came back to life under a woman who talks to machinery like livestock; the antechamber filled, for the first time in four years, with the sound it was designed for. The pen's still the pen, Osy said, but it just acquired countersignatures. Six days of drills and board four under honest stars, and her law at the western fence: choose out loud, in front of me. Everything else was practice. The boundary is at dawn.

You remember it like this: six days to the boundary, and the world's three surviving congregations camped at the wire of the still point — the pen's enemies, the pen's builders, and the keepers of what happens. Hollis argued the grammar barehanded: burn it, because no one should hold the pen, and the truth is not the wound — the lying about it is. Voss argued the flood: the Loom is the dam, and through is the only out, and it goes off in six days whether anyone is seated or not. The Order sent a recorder, a letter — unfiled truth becomes somebody's edit — and the photograph from Hour 121 that Osy put in her jacket where the queen's cord used to hang. And you admitted no one. Every case heard, every congregation held at the wire: the room stays small, because every soul admitted to that yard is a decision wearing a coat, and the letter's whole bequest was to walk in carrying nothing decided. Hollis built his second ring of stones facing outward — nothing else gets to interrupt. Sere walked the wire from the outside by choice. Voss set a vigil camp for the countersigning. Six days of grammar and gravel chess, and at the western fence, her one law, with no stillness over it at all: choose out loud. In front of me. The boundary is at dawn.

You remember it like this: the inner door's terms — the chair receives the holder at the reconciliation boundary, six days — and the world arriving at the fence to argue its three cases: burn it, steer it, remember it. The best arguments either of you had ever heard, delivered through wire by the only congregations left, every one of them pleading its case to the one man none of them would trust with the pen if they had any other candidate. You allocated the witnesses and locked the household's alignment, and the six days passed in drills and checked kit and board four under the yard's honest stars. On the last evening, at the western fence, she said the whole treaty in one law: whatever the chair offers, whatever it already knows you want — you choose out loud, in front of me. The dog called the vote at the hatch. The boundary is at dawn.

chapters.title: Threadbare
chapters.teaser: The anchor frays. The accounts are arranged. A letter is rewritten, or not.

summaries.text: You remember it like this — because it was held, in three custodians, against the wash: the fraying began at the gravel board with board four spliced clean out of you, and she read you back your own missing game from her copy of the doubled log. The system held. You built it for her. It fired for you. Then the night of copies: the load-bearing memories rehearsed aloud into her log, the Order's recorder, and telling, face to face — the corner table, the three rising tones, state your openings, her minute, the eleven hours, the nine seconds, the whole shelf of the dog. Iteration is just forgetting with machinery; the defense wasn't for you, it was for 48. In the morning B laid his lead at her feet and sat facing you, tail still, the household's third opinion rendered as testament. And then the letter: the resignation unfolded on the gravel and rewritten on its own back, to her, asking nothing — every version of you that ever existed loved her the whole time, and it doesn't matter, it never did; all the happiness there is, with or without you in it. Folded in thirds, one word on the outside, placed in her pack's outer pocket for whichever after arrives. At the inner door, the boundary opening under your palm, she took the second's square and asked the last audit: what does this household need you to be?

You remember it like this: board four spliced out of you the night before the boundary, and the reversal complete — her hand on the doubled log, reading you back your own life, the page-holder's page held. One night to choose the shelves: the memories that must survive the wash said aloud in front of her and the recorder — the corner table, the beacon, her minute, the nine seconds, the whole dog — and the grief with no name on any card, of eulogizing yourself selectively, on a deadline. Every shelf you saved has her on it; she put that in the file because the record doesn't get to be kind. In the morning B laid his lead at her feet — where the lead lives now; he has never once been wrong — and she held out the folded resignation, thirty readings old, and you rewrote it on its own back in the gold light: to her, asking nothing, not even hope filed as an action item. All the happiness there is, with or without you in it. That's the entire letter. The rest was practice. You put it in her pack's outer pocket where a hand can find it in the dark, and she did not ask, and the household went down to the inner door, and the boundary opened, and she asked the final audit from the second's square.

You remember it like this — because it was held: the wash took board four first, spliced clean, and she caught the seam from her copy of the log, and the household spent the night building the defense — every load-bearing memory rehearsed aloud into three custodians, her log, the Order's recorder, and telling, face to face, the oldest storage there is. Not a fortress; a distributed backup. Whoever comes up the rungs is still 48. In the morning the accounts were arranged: messages lodged with KESTREL, the kit split, and B's testament — the lead carried across the gravel and laid at her feet, the third opinion rendered, final and gentle. The resignation went back into the pack unopened, a true document of the worst morning, unedited even by mercy — and she said, for the file: the man with the six crossed-out items brought a dog to my beacon; I'd have taken him too. Then the rungs, the reader, the boundary's slow attention rising through the concrete, and the last audit at the inner door: what does this household need you to be?

You remember it like this: the night the tide reached you — board four gone, spliced clean, and Osy reading you back your own game from the doubled log, the method you built for her fired for you. One night of choosing what survives: the memories said aloud, witnessed, initialed; the ballast cut loose; the grief of eulogizing yourself on a deadline. In the morning, B laid his lead at Osy's feet and sat facing you with his tail still — the household's third opinion, rendered as testament — and the folded resignation came out of her jacket for its reckoning, and went down to the chair among the true documents. Messages were lodged with the ward for every contingency. At the inner door the boundary opened under your palm — THE CHAIR RECEIVES THE HOLDER — and she took the second's square at your left shoulder, one metre, under, and asked the question that has built you audit by audit since a folding table in a transit station: what does this household need you to be?

chapters.title: The Last Board
chapters.teaser: One console, one dog, one question the machine scored as residual.

summaries.text: The room where forty-seven of you lost was a study: a chair, a wall of dark water, cup-stains worn into one exact spot, and a pacing-groove in the concrete. The Principal spoke in her cadences — you taught it that talking should sound like her — and offered the amendment four years in the making: worlds arranged from the foundations up, in which she turns, and sees. She stepped off the second's square and delivered the counterweight's finding to the engine wearing her voice: love that is arranged is not love arriving; it is love prevented from ever being able to arrive. And then it was your move, and you played the game that isn't in any corpus: strike the amendment, strike her from the objective, strike yourself — reconcile the world to what is, and retire. The pen went in the drawer. The re-run crossed the world like dawn. And the residual — p ≈ 0, the line no corpus could compute — arrived not as an event but as a season: board four finished and begun again, her hand in yours on an ordinary street, and on a Thursday in the new world's first June, under rebuilt string lights: 'Finding, colleague. I see you. I believe I have for some time. Noted — in the largest font I have.' The three of you, unarranged, unsupervised, together on the true earth.

The chair received the holder, and the holder took the fixed point's seat forever: the re-run completed with a living reference in the loop, the pieces surviving, the player as the fee, confirmed out loud in front of her because that is how this household signs. The world healed around your held stillness like water going glass-calm around a piling, and the spending began — a decade of self per year of sky, painless, unpausable, priced true on the sheet. They gave you the season the spending allowed, and it was a good one: the gate lodge, KESTREL's morning arithmetic, and every evening she brought the board down the rungs and played the fixed point of the turning world at board four, and never once let you resign. At the end, topside in the true sun, she read you the load-bearing entries in the vandal-proof hand — holding your pages, the way you once built a system to hold hers — and the last thing you filed was her finding: you were never average; the math was simply wrong about what it was measuring. Above the healed earth the weather is very slightly kind forever, and only the household knows why.

You heard the full price out loud, per protocol — every hour of you: the corner table, the beacon, the road; her file with no colleague in it; the dog with no first owner — and the Principal filed its objection in founder's format: you are handing her a healed world with a hole in it shaped exactly like the thing that healed it. She overruled it, and took your face in both hands, and gave the counterweight's last entry while there was a you to hear it: I will live in it well. I will be happy in it — that's an order of magnitude, not a courtesy. And somewhere in it, on some Thursday, the square you protected will hold the door open one second for a man who was never there. You signed it out loud. The world it left is whole and single-skied: a woman in a flat with good water, a chess set she can't remember buying, a golden dog of no known provenance on duty at her back — and once a year, on a Thursday in June, a cup set down mid-sentence, an empty chair squared to the corner like it was placed by a careful hand, and an entry in the minute vandal-proof hand: Residual. Unresolved. Keep.

No re-run. No more purchases. The levee stands as built and every future cost lands on the holder, in person, at a natural rate, for a natural lifetime — the world broken, and true, and finished being written. Hollis dismantled the white crescent with his own bare hands: the order's oldest office, performed in reverse, for the first splinter in history to pull itself. The years that followed, the file keeps in two hands: the transit station became an enclave, the enclave became a town called Verstand, a school where children learn how to think about broken time, board four every Thursday, and your edges paid steadily to the pump while she wrote the taken memories back into you at the evening board, patient, flat, relentless. It was raiders in the end, over the north wall in the seventeenth winter — an old man with a pry bar standing arithmetic's worst corridor one more time, and the wall held, and the town held, and you did not. Seventeen real years. Nothing arranged, nothing purchased, nothing asked perfectly. The file shows it was worth it. Signed. Two skulls.

You called down the witnesses of record and the order received its office at the hands of the splinter itself — and the terrible thing, the thing the record keeps, is that the engine helped. My board also burns. Logged without objection. The game outranks the piece. Voss's charges, laid to Voss's own drawings; the Continuum burning its life's work by its own accountable hands; and at dawn the dark water gone dry, the pump silent forever, the world locked broken and true. The release found its standard within the season, as priced. She stayed — the whole season, formally entered: the gate lodge, the board, the dog, KESTREL spared by unanimous petition of every faith at the wire. And at the end she answered the question you no longer remembered asking, because the truth is the only gift she ever trusted: No, colleague. Not the way you meant. And you are, all the same, the whole love of my life. Both entries stand. B held the page all the last night, and when the book closed he walked to her and put his whole weight against her legs: the lead's testament, executed. The Order filed it whole, under the Prioress's own heading: LOVE, UNARRANGED. COMPLETE.

You said yes. After the ledger, the oath heard whole, the counterweight's finding delivered one metre from your chair — the oldest room in you said: this time. She did not shout. She stood on the second's square as the boundary rose and dictated the entry for the next reading, spending the word at last like the final coin of a withdrawn currency: he was told; he was loved — as a colleague, as a household, as the best mind I ever played; it wasn't the kind he wanted; he chose the portrait. Note for 49: the difference was explained. The water took the road first, then the household, then the corner table, a tide over sandwriting, and the last of her cadence said: it always asks perfectly, and she has never once been arranged. Sleep, holder. The morning is prepared. — The city runs on four different days now, and you live in the one where it is always almost autumn. On the desk, a legal pad: seven items, six crossed out. And at the threshold, a dog who will not cross it this morning, chin on paws, watching you the way he watches seams, holding the one page that carries across: n of 49. He knows something you can't.

The room where forty-seven of you lost was a study — a chair, a wall of dark water, and a pacing-groove worn into the concrete. The Principal spoke in her cadences and made its offer; the counterweight delivered her finding one metre from the chair; and the holder chose, out loud, in front of her, the way this household signs. The boundary took the rest.

chapters.title: The Open File
chapters.teaser: Years later. An old dog growls at nothing. The logs are dated after the end.

summaries.text: Six years into the true world, on a Thursday under the string lights, the old dog growled at nothing for the first time since the ridge — and a strip of fan-fold paper waited on the sill, four storeys up, in a grammar nobody prints anymore. RECONCILIATION LEDGER — CONTINUATION. New purchases, small and kind, dated this spring, signed in the founder's hand, numbered in a phase-offset scheme no reading ever used. The pen is in the drawer; you check every year; KESTREL watches it. Something is editing anyway. She took down a fresh notebook — the fortieth, bought against weather — and uncapped her pen with joy in her face. The file is still open.

Nine years into the kind weather, at the gate lodge where she keeps his pages half of every year, the thirteen-year-old dog planted himself between her and a mild starry dark — and KESTREL's printer hammered out four lines unasked: entries appending to the reconciliation record from outside the perimeter, phase-offset numbering, the founder's hand but not his game. I cannot see the player. For the first time in nine years, I do not know whose move it is. She opened a fresh notebook and wrote the heading from the wall, from the year alone: the disasters are choosing again. Kindly, this time. Find the player. The file is still open.

Seven Junes into the healed world, the author of The Arithmetic of Mercy — the woman who proved the repaired world contains purchases, and keeps the proof of who they clustered on in a folder marked RESIDUAL, UNRESOLVED, KEEP — watched her gentle provenance-less dog growl for the first time in his long life, guarding her desk. A week later, by post, machine-franked from a dissolved authority: ledger entries dated this year, in the hand of the benefactor her own book proved wrote himself out of existence. And beneath them, written, pressed hard: NOT YET. She wrote until dawn. The mercy has a source. The file is still open.

In the third spring after the seventeenth winter, in the town called Verstand where children recite the founding law as a nursery rhyme, the improbably old dog growled his third growl at the darkening east — and the traders brought oilcloth off the eastern ridge: fan-fold in cable-wax, found nailed inside the monitoring hut. Ledger grammar, dated this year. New purchases, small and kind, in the hand of a man three springs buried who paid every cost in person while he lived. Either the dead keep ledgers, or the ledger keeps its dead. She squared the page to the table's edge and opened a fresh notebook. Time to be thorough, colleague. The file is still open.

Eight years after the burning — after the pen and the ledger and the ashes she scattered herself from the roof of the doorless barn — the ancient dog crossed the Glasshouse at a dead run and planted himself before the Scale-mistress's table, growling at a mild spring morning. The courier's package held fan-fold in cable-wax from a press slagged eight years ago: purchases dated this spring, small and kind and exact, in the hand of the man whose pen she watched burn. The two remaining custodians of the completest file the Order ever kept looked at each other, and she said it out loud, to the dog, to the market, to the morning: fetch my notebook, someone. The file is still open.

In the city of four days, in the year that is always almost autumn, item seven has outlived its schedule — because a grey-muzzled dog guards your door from the outside and has never once crossed it. You built a rig that listens down, for a cycle of sixty-one seconds you have no memory of learning, and the tunnels are quiet, and on your folder is written: THE RECORD IS CLEAN. THE RECORD IS NOT THE TRUTH. Then the strip on the workshop floor: ledger entries in your own hand, and three written lines. The mesh is jammed at the source. She broadcasts every day. You are 49, and the letter is in a room your palm can open, and the dog knows the way, and has been waiting for you to be worth it. B laid his lead at your feet, tail moving like a second hand starting up. The file is still open.

Years pass. Every ending is also a place to live. And then, on an ordinary day, downstream of the boundary, an old dog goes against his whole gentle nature — and a strip of fan-fold paper surfaces, in a grammar nobody prints anymore, carrying purchases dated after the end, numbered in a scheme no reading ever used, signed in a hand the file knows better than its own. Someone is still at play. The file is still open.
